content.content:
content: '# Web Document


  ## Metadata


  - title: Information for Health Care Providers to Complete a Certification under
  the FMLA

  - url: https://www.dol.gov/agencies/whd/fmla/certification-of-a-serious-health-condition

  - source: U.S. Department of Labor, Wage and Hour Division

  - domain: dol.gov


  ## Content


  # Information for Health Care Providers to Complete a Certification under the FMLA


  The Family and Medical Leave Act (FMLA) allows eligible employees to take job-protected
  leave for qualifying family and medical reasons. Leave can be taken in one continuous
  block, intermittently, or on a reduced schedule when there is a medical need. An
  employer may require a medical certification to verify the need for leave connected
  to an employee''s own serious health condition or a family member''s serious health
  condition.


  ## Supporting an FMLA leave request


  A medical certification should provide the information needed to support the employee''s
  need for leave. Depending on the situation, a health care provider may be asked
  for an initial certification, a later recertification, a second or third opinion
  when permitted, or a fitness-for-duty certification.


  For an employee''s own serious health condition, a complete certification generally
  addresses:


  - the health care provider''s contact and practice information;

  - when the serious health condition began and how long it is expected to last;

  - whether the employee is unable to perform one or more essential job functions
  and, if so, for how long; and

  - appropriate medical facts showing the need for leave.


  For leave involving care of a family member, the certification may also address
  whether care is needed and provide an estimate of the frequency and duration of
  that care.


  ## Intermittent or reduced-schedule leave


  When leave is medically necessary in separate blocks of time or on a reduced schedule,
  the certification should estimate the employee''s expected leave pattern. The relevant
  information includes:


  - how much time may be needed for each absence;

  - how often absences may occur; and

  - information showing why intermittent or reduced-schedule leave is medically necessary.


  The estimate should reflect the health care provider''s best-informed medical judgment.
  An exact schedule is not required when the need for leave is unpredictable.


  A provider may include other appropriate medical facts, such as a diagnosis, symptoms,
  or a continuing-treatment regimen, but those additional details are not automatically
  required. A diagnosis is not necessary merely because a certification concerns a
  serious health condition.


  ## Follow-up on incomplete or unclear information


  A provider may be asked to clarify information that is vague, unclear, or nonresponsive,
  or to supply information required for a complete certification that was not provided.
  The follow-up should address the actual missing or unclear item.


  Once an employer has received a complete and sufficient certification, it generally
  may not request additional medical information from the provider or ask the employee
  to obtain more. The employer may use authorized personnel to authenticate or clarify
  the certification, and in some recertification situations may ask whether an absence
  pattern is consistent with the certified need for leave.


  ## Protecting patient privacy


  A certification does not need to disclose the patient''s diagnosis. It should provide
  appropriate medical facts showing that the employee needs leave for an FMLA-protected
  health condition.


  The FMLA does not require an employee to sign a broad release of medical information.
  Whether to authorize direct communication between the employer and the health care
  provider is the employee''s choice. The health care provider only needs to provide
  health information that relates to the employee''s need for leave from work.


  Information about genetic tests, genetic services, or the manifestation of disease
  or disorder in family members should not be included. State or local law may also
  limit disclosure of private medical information, including diagnosis or course of
  treatment.


  ## Forms and completed certifications


  The FMLA does not require use of a particular certification form. Employers may
  use their own forms if they meet legal requirements, and the Department of Labor
  provides optional-use forms.


  A completed certification should be returned to the employee for delivery to the
  employer, unless the employee authorizes the health care provider to send it directly
  to the employer. Completed certifications should not be sent to the U.S. Department
  of Labor.

  '

content: "# Web Document

## Metadata

- title: Certification of Health Care Provider\
  \ for Employee’s Serious Health Condition under the Family and Medical Leave\
  \ Act - WH-380-E
- url: https://www.dol.gov/sites/dolgov/files/WHD/legacy/files/WH-380-E.pdf
\
  - source: U.S. Department of Labor, Wage and Hour Division
- domain: dol.gov

\
  ## Content

# Certification of Health Care Provider for Employee’s Serious\
  \ Health Condition under the Family and Medical Leave Act - WH-380-E

## Part\
  \ B: Amount of Leave Needed

For the medical condition or conditions identified\
  \ in Part A, the health care provider completes all Part B questions that apply.\
  \ Several questions ask for an estimate of the frequency or duration of a condition,\
  \ treatment, or period of incapacity. Answers should be the provider’s best\
  \ estimate based on medical knowledge, experience, and examination of the patient.\
  \ The form asks the provider to be as specific as possible.

### Item 5 —\
  \ Planned medical treatments

When the employee has planned medical treatments\
  \ or scheduled medical visits, the provider identifies the applicable treatment\
  \ dates.

### Item 6 — Referral to other health care providers

When the\
  \ employee is referred to another health care provider for evaluation or treatment,\
  \ the provider states the nature of the treatment and gives a best estimate of its\
  \ beginning date, end date, and duration, including any period of recovery.

\
  ### Item 7 — Reduced schedule

When it is medically necessary for the employee\
  \ to work a reduced schedule, the provider gives a best estimate of the reduced\
  \ schedule the employee is able to work. The form asks for the period during which\
  \ the reduced schedule applies and the estimated work schedule, such as a number\
  \ of hours per day or hours per week.

### Item 8 — Continuous period of\
  \ incapacity

When the employee is or will be incapacitated for a continuous period\
  \ of time, including time for treatment or recovery, the provider gives a best estimate\
  \ of the beginning and end dates of that continuous period.

### Item 9 —\
  \ Intermittent episodes of incapacity

When it is medically necessary for the\
  \ employee to be absent from work on an intermittent basis, including for episodic\
  \ flare-ups, the provider gives a best estimate of:

- how often the episodes\
  \ of incapacity are likely to occur; and
- how long each episode is likely to last.
\
  
For the next six months, the form provides spaces to estimate the number of episodes\
  \ per day, week, or month and the approximate number of hours or days per episode.
\
  
The form separates these different leave-amount questions because the applicable\
  \ information depends on the kind of leave need being certified. Only the relevant\
  \ Part B items are completed for the medical condition identified in Part A.
"
metadata: {}
title: "Certification of Health Care Provider for Employee’s Serious Health Condition\
  \ under the Family and Medical Leave Act - WH-380-E"
url: https://www.dol.gov/sites/dolgov/files/WHD/legacy/files/WH-380-E.pdf

Your medical-certification case **MLC-2741** is active, and clarification is pending for **Form WH-380-E, Part B, Item 9**.

Because your leave is recorded as **intermittent or reduced-schedule leave**, your health-care provider needs to give a best estimate for the next six months of:

- **How often** incapacity episodes are likely to occur — per day, week, or month; and
- **How long each episode** is likely to last — approximately how many hours or days per episode.

The provider should return the clarified certification to you for submission, unless you have authorized the provider to send it directly to your employer. An exact schedule is not required when the need is unpredictable, and a diagnosis generally is not required solely for this item. These requirements are based on the U.S. Department of Labor’s WH-380-E form and FMLA guidance.

final_answer:
Your medical-certification case **MLC-2741** is active, and clarification is pending for **Form WH-380-E, Part B, Item 9**.

Because your leave is recorded as **intermittent or reduced-schedule leave**, your health-care provider needs to give a best estimate for the next six months of:

- **How often** incapacity episodes are likely to occur — per day, week, or month; and
- **How long each episode** is likely to last — approximately how many hours or days per episode.

The provider should return the clarified certification to you for submission, unless you have authorized the provider to send it directly to your employer. An exact schedule is not required when the need is unpredictable, and a diagnosis generally is not required solely for this item. These requirements are based on the U.S. Department of Labor’s WH-380-E form and FMLA guidance.
